The Bayon Temple complex was built under the direction of the Mahayana Buddhist ruler Jayavarman VII, who ascended to the Khmer kingdom's throne at Angkor in 1181 A.D. He erected the site for Buddhist worship, although it later was renovated and used as a Hindu temple. Various forms of Hindu and Buddhist worship were practiced side-by-side and successively in the ancient royal courts of Southeast Asia. Classical Khmer kings promoted the idea (known as "devaraja") that there was an intersection of the ruling king and a validating god. Usually the Hindu god chosen for this personal identification was Siva, but sometimes it was Vishnu, or, for some, a godly image of Buddhist origins. Khmer temples thus often portray the ruling king

title: Angkor Thom

The south gate of Angkor Thom is 7.2 km (4.5 mi) north of Siem Reap and 1.7 km (1.1 mi) north of the entrance to Angkor Wat. The walls, 8 m (26 ft) high and flanked by a moat, are each 3 km (1.9 mi) long, enclosing an area of 9 km2 (3.5 sq mi). The walls are of laterite buttressed by earth, with a parapet on the top. The faces on the 23 m (75 ft) towers at the city gates, which are later additions to the main structure, take after those of the Bayon and pose the same problems of interpretation.
